title: if-else Statements
description: Handle both True and False cases.

slides.title: Two Paths 🔀
slides.content:
# When You Need Plan B

Sometimes you want to do something else when the condition is False:

```python
if condition:
    do_this()
else:
    do_that()
```

Only ONE of these blocks will run!

slides.title: Basic if-else 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
age = 16

if age >= 18:
    print("You can vote!")
else:
    print("You're too young to vote")
    years_left = 18 - age
    print(f"Come back in {years_left} years!")

quizData.question: How many blocks run in an if-else?
quizData.explanation: In if-else, exactly ONE block runs — either the if block (when True) or the else block (when False)!

slides.title: else Syntax 📝
slides.content:
# The else Keyword

```python
if condition:
    # Runs if True
    statement1
else:
    # Runs if False
    statement2
```

* `else` is on its own line
* Followed by a colon `:`
* Block is indented

slides.title: Practical Examples 💻

quizData.question: What comes after 'else'?
quizData.explanation: 'else' doesn't need a condition — it handles all cases where the if condition was False!

slides.title: The Ternary Operator 🎯
slides.content:
# One-Line Conditionals

Python has a shorthand for simple if-else:

```python
# Instead of:
if age >= 18:
    status = "adult"
else:
    status = "minor"

# Write:
status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
```

slides.title: Ternary Examples 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
age = 20

# Ternary operator
status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
print(f"Status: {status}")

# In a print statement
print("Even" if age % 2 == 0 else "Odd")

# Setting values
score = 85
grade = "Pass" if score >= 70 else "Fail"
print(f"Grade: {grade}")

quizData.question: What is: x = 5 if True else 10?
quizData.explanation: The condition is True, so x gets the first value: 5!

slides.title: Choosing the Right Style 🎨
slides.content:
# When to Use Which?

**Use multi-line if-else when:**
* Multiple statements in blocks
* Complex logic
* Need readability

**Use ternary when:**
* Simple value assignment
* Short, clear conditions
* One expression each side

slides.title: Ternary Overuse ⚠️
slides.content:
## Don't Overdo It!

**Bad:** Too complex
```python
x = a if b else c if d else e
```

**Good:** Break it down
```python
if b:
    x = a
elif d:
    x = c
else:
    x = e
```

slides.title: Real-World Example 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
# Simple login check
username = "admin"
password = "secret123"

if username == "admin" and password == "secret123":
    print("Login successful!")
    print("Welcome to the dashboard")
else:
    print("Login failed!")
    print("Please check your credentials")

quizData.question: If username is wrong but password is correct, what happens?
quizData.explanation: Both conditions must be True (and). If username is wrong, the whole condition is False → else block runs!

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Default Values

Use 'or' for default values:

```python
username = input_name or "Guest"
# If input_name is empty/None,
# username becomes "Guest"
```

This is a common Python pattern!

quizData.question: In 'x = a if cond else b', when is b assigned?
quizData.explanation: b is assigned when cond is False — otherwise a is assigned!
